content.body: Andreu Botella: that could be better formalized I think
content.formatted_body: Andreu Botella: that could be better formalized I think

content.body: Well, when I'm reading a spec casually I appreciate things like that, rather than having to parse out the meaning of the boilerplate
but here I was trying to figure out whether those were run synchronously when the element is inserted, the attributes changes and so on...
Andreu Botella: file a spec issue :) I'm guessing sync since the spec doesn't say to queue a task or anything, but I'm not sure what is implemented
content.formatted_body: Andreu Botella: file a spec issue :) I'm guessing sync since the spec doesn't say to queue a task or anything, but I'm not sure what is implemented

content.body: In most cases the side effects of this either queue a task (e.g. to fire an `error` event) or perform other async operations like fetching, so it shouldn't be observable whether this is sync or async
content.formatted_body: In most cases the side effects of this either queue a task (e.g. to fire an error event) or perform other async operations like fetching, so it shouldn't be observable whether this is sync or async

content.body: ... would be good to find if there are cases where the difference here is observable in any way
Of course, formalizing it better would be a net win
yeah, it probably isn't observable, but if they're sync that would help for AsyncContext
with AsyncContext, you can have state that is preserved across async propagations, such as awaits, or timers
or things like the propagation between inserting a `` or `` element and having the `load` event fired
content.formatted_body: or things like the propagation between inserting a <link> or <img> element and having the load event fired

content.body: so if the fetch and processing is triggered by inserion, the state would have to be saved synchronously when the element is inserted
Andreu Botella: those steps usually say "when the link element becomes browsing context connected" (e.g. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#link-type-preload:becomes-browsing-context-connected)
content.formatted_body: Andreu Botella: those steps usually say "when the link element becomes browsing context connected" (e.g. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#link-type-preload:becomes-browsing-context-connected)

content.body: Which is specifically the insertion steps into a connected tree: https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#becomes-browsing-context-connected
so I think it's already formally synchronous. Though of course can be spec'ed explicitly as "the link element's insertion steps" (but it wouldn't be normatively different)
I should check if that is the case for all of the other possible cases, such as modifying an attribute and so on
